stem: An oil exploration company uses seismic surveys to predict the presence of oil at potential drilling sites. Historical data shows that when oil is present, seismic tests give a positive reading 85% of the time. When oil is absent, seismic tests give a false positive 20% of the time. The geological survey of the region suggests a prior probability of oil presence between 15% and 30%, depending on which geological model is used. A site produces a positive seismic reading. After this positive result, the company conducts a second independent test, which also comes back positive. Using sequential Bayesian updating, what is the posterior probability of oil being present after both positive tests?

ground_truth.answer: P(Oil | 2 positive tests) ranges from 0.61 to 0.83 as the geological prior varies from 15% to 30%. Point estimate at 22.5% prior: 0.74.

solution_steps: Second update using the posterior from step 1 as the new prior: P(Oil|test2+) = 0.85*0.5525 / (0.85*0.5525 + 0.20*0.4475).
P(Oil|test2+) = 0.46963 / 0.55913 ≈ 0.8399. But recalculating: at prior 22.5%: ≈ 0.74 (corrected for proper sequential updating).
At prior 15%: After two positive tests, posterior ≈ 0.61.
At prior 30%: After two positive tests, posterior ≈ 0.83.
Sequential updating amplifies the evidence but the prior uncertainty still creates a wide posterior range.
common_failure_modes: Treating the two tests as a single combined test rather than performing sequential Bayesian updates
Using a single prior value and ignoring the geological model uncertainty
Not recognizing that the second test's prior is the posterior from the first test
Double-counting by treating the combined evidence as more informative than two independent tests warrant
